terms.definition: A sophisticated virtual replica of a physical asset that synchronizes data across different levels of accuracy and computational cost. It combines low-fidelity, fast-running simulations with high-fidelity, sensor-rich data to diagnose complex system faults where real-world failure data is historically scarce.
terms.analogy: In a kitchen, this is like using a cheap, instant-read thermometer for 90% of your cooking, but switching to a $10,000 laboratory-grade thermal scanner the moment the soufflé starts to vibrate, ensuring precision only when the stakes are highest.
terms.business_value: For capital-intensive industries like aviation or manufacturing, this technology enables 'predictive maintenance' for rare, catastrophic failures that traditional AI models miss, significantly reducing unplanned downtime and liability costs.

terms.definition: A reasoning architecture where an AI agent generates and executes multiple potential solutions or code paths simultaneously rather than following a single linear thought process. These paths are then cross-referenced against automated test suites to identify the most accurate output, effectively treating AI reasoning as a software debugging task.
terms.analogy: Imagine a car's GPS that doesn't just suggest one route, but simultaneously sends out 10 'ghost cars' to drive every possible shortcut in real-time, instantly reporting back which path actually has a hidden road closure before you even turn the steering wheel.
terms.business_value: This breaks the trade-off between speed and accuracy. It allows CEOs to deploy AI for high-stakes financial or data tasks (like Text-to-SQL) where 'almost right' is a failure, ensuring the model self-corrects before the user ever sees the data.

terms.definition: The process of using LLMs to translate 'informal' human reasoning—such as scientific papers or handwritten proofs—into 'formal' computer-verifiable code (e.g., Lean or Coq). This transforms abstract, ambiguous human ideas into rigid mathematical structures that a computer can prove to be 100% logically correct.
terms.analogy: It is like a Master Chef taking a grandmother’s vague handwritten recipe that says 'add a pinch of love' and 'cook until it looks right,' and instantly translating it into a precise, molecular-gastronomy blueprint that a robotic kitchen can execute perfectly every single time.
terms.business_value: Autoformalisation allows enterprises to turn 'tribal knowledge' and unstructured R&D into verifiable, executable assets. It bridges the gap between creative human innovation and the zero-error tolerance required for mission-critical software and scientific discovery.
